issues.description: File::from_raw_fd(fd) takes ownership of the file descriptor. When the BufWriter (and its inner File) drops at the end of the function, the fd is closed. The docstring promises 'the caller is responsible for closing the fd when done' and the trailing comment acknowledges the bug ('the caller expects the fd to remain open'). Any caller that subsequently reads from, writes to, or closes this fd will trigger undefined behavior (EBADF or, worse, operating on a recycled fd). This is a classic ownership-transfer footgun with FromRawFd.
issues.suggested_fix: Use File::from_raw_fd to create the File, then after flushing call file.into_raw_fd() (via BufWriter::into_inner()) to release ownership back to the caller without closing the fd. For example: `let file = writer.into_inner().map_err(|e| e.into_error())?; let _ = file.into_raw_fd();`

issues.description: mesh.triangles() does not exist. The Mesh struct (reify-types/src/geometry.rs:197-204) has a public field `indices: Vec<u32>`, not a method called triangles(). This will fail to compile.

mesh.vertices() does not exist as a method. The Mesh struct has a public field `vertices: Vec<f32>`. Even if this were changed to `&mesh.vertices`, the subsequent code treats `vertices[idx as usize]` as a struct with `.x`, `.y`, `.z` fields, but vertices is a flat Vec<f32> laid out as [x0, y0, z0, x1, y1, z1, ...]. Indexing it by a triangle index gives a single f32, not a vertex struct. This will fail to compile.
issues.suggested_fix: Access flat vertices by computing the base offset: `let base = (idx as usize) * 3; writer.write_all(&vertices[base].to_le_bytes())?; writer.write_all(&vertices[base + 1].to_le_bytes())?; writer.write_all(&vertices[base + 2].to_le_bytes())?;`. Also remove the `as f32` casts since vertices are already f32.

issues.description: The code accesses v.x, v.y, v.z on what would be an f32 value (from the flat vertex array) and casts them `as f32`, indicating the author incorrectly assumed vertices are f64 structs. Mesh::vertices is Vec<f32> in flat [x,y,z,...] layout. This entire vertex access pattern is wrong and will not compile.
issues.suggested_fix: See the fix for the vertex access pattern above — index into the flat array at base+0, base+1, base+2 and write the f32 values directly without casting.

issues.description: std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd is Unix-only. This function (and the import at line 4) will cause compile failures on non-Unix platforms. If cross-platform support is needed, consider using std::os::fd::FromRawFd (stabilized in Rust 1.66) with a cfg gate, or accept a generic Write trait object instead of a raw fd.
issues.suggested_fix: Gate the function with #[cfg(unix)] or, preferably, refactor to accept `writer: &mut dyn Write` which is platform-agnostic and consistent with the GeometryKernel::export signature already used in the codebase.

issues.description: Casting `triangles.len() / 3` to u32 could silently truncate if the mesh has more than ~4 billion triangles (unlikely but no explicit check). More practically, if indices.len() is not a multiple of 3, the division silently drops remainder triangles with no warning.
issues.suggested_fix: Add a debug_assert or check that `mesh.indices.len() % 3 == 0` and use u32::try_from() for the triangle count conversion.

issues.description: No bounds checking on vertex indices before array access. If the mesh contains an out-of-range index in `indices`, the flat-array access (once corrected) would panic at runtime. Malformed mesh data from tessellation bugs could trigger this.
issues.suggested_fix: Validate that all indices are within bounds (idx * 3 + 2 < vertices.len()) before the write loop, or use .get() with an error return.

opus_bug_hunter.summary: This function has multiple blocking compilation errors and a critical runtime bug. The code assumes Mesh has vertex() and triangles() methods returning structured vertex objects, but Mesh actually has public fields `vertices: Vec<f32>` (flat [x,y,z,...] layout) and `indices: Vec<u32>`. The vertex access pattern (v.x, v.y, v.z with f64→f32 cast) is entirely wrong for the flat f32 array. Additionally, File::from_raw_fd takes ownership of the fd and closes it on drop, contradicting the documented contract that the caller retains ownership. The function cannot compile as written, and even after fixing the type issues, the fd-closing bug would cause use-after-close in any caller. Consider refactoring to accept `&mut dyn Write` instead of a raw fd, consistent with existing codebase patterns.

issues.description: File::from_raw_fd takes ownership of the fd, and the BufWriter<File> drop at function exit closes it. The doc comment says 'the caller is responsible for closing the fd when done', but the fd is already closed when Ok(bytes_written) is returned. The inline comment even acknowledges this: 'But the caller expects the fd to remain open for further operations.' This is a runtime bug — the caller will operate on a closed fd.
issues.suggested_fix: After writer.flush(), recover the File via writer.into_inner() and then call file.into_raw_fd() (from std::os::unix::io::IntoRawFd) to relinquish ownership without closing. Alternatively, wrap the File in ManuallyDrop to prevent the close-on-drop.

issues.description: The STL binary writing logic (80-byte header, triangle count, per-triangle vertex serialization with attribute byte count) is duplicated between export_stl and export_stl_to_fd. Both functions implement the same binary format byte-for-byte.
issues.suggested_fix: Extract a shared helper fn write_stl_binary(writer: &mut impl Write, mesh: &Mesh) -> Result<usize> that both export_stl and export_stl_to_fd call. This eliminates duplication and ensures format changes only need to happen in one place.

issues.description: std::os::unix::io::FromRawFd is unix-only. Without a #[cfg(unix)] gate on the import and function, this will fail to compile on non-unix targets. The existing export_stl is platform-agnostic.
issues.suggested_fix: Gate the import and export_stl_to_fd function behind #[cfg(unix)] or use std::os::fd::FromRawFd (stabilized in Rust 1.66+) with appropriate cfg guards. If Windows support isn't needed, at minimum add #[cfg(unix)] so the intent is explicit.

issues.description: The SAFETY comment only says 'caller guarantees fd is valid and open', but the real invariant for from_raw_fd is that the fd must not be owned by anything else (i.e., no other File or resource will close it). The comment should document the full ownership transfer semantics, especially since the current code violates the caller contract by closing the fd.
issues.suggested_fix: Expand the SAFETY comment to cover: (1) fd is valid and open, (2) no other owner will close it, (3) this function takes (or borrows) ownership. This also ties into the blocking fd-close fix — once fixed, the safety invariants should match the actual behavior.

opus_design_critic.summary: The new export_stl_to_fd function has a blocking fd-ownership bug: File::from_raw_fd takes ownership of the file descriptor, and the implicit drop at function exit closes it — directly contradicting the documented contract that says the caller is responsible for closing. The code even has a comment acknowledging this problem without fixing it. Beyond the blocker, the STL binary format writing logic is fully duplicated from export_stl and should be extracted into a shared generic helper. The unix-only import also needs a cfg gate for portability clarity.
